steps.instruction: Placement of the Coded Marker (Ground Control Point - GCP) Position the GCP on the rock art panel, ensuring an even distribution along the panel's outer rim. Employ an adhesive that leaves no residue on the panel but is sufficiently strong to secure the marker during the image capture process. Refrain from positioning the marker on or too close to the rock paintings.

Total Station Setup Ideally, establish the total station using the geodetic datum if one is already available at the site. If not, utilize the local coordinate system and perform the necessary calibration.

LED Light Setup In cases where natural light inadequately illuminates the panel, position several LED lights to evenly light the rock art panel. Adjust the light intensity to prevent shadow casting on the panel.
Recording Marker Coordinates Document the coordinates of the marker using the total station and store it as 'target n ', where ' n ' represents the coded marker's number.

Inner Panel Coordinate Capture Record coordinates at select points within the panel's inner area, prioritizing points with clear and sharp edges. Remember to photograph the point while the total station's laser is still active. This photograph will assist in accurately positioning the GCP during photogrammetry image processing.
Camera Setup
Focal Length: If using a zoom lens, fix the focal length to its shortest distance. Maintain this focal length across all images.

File Format: Configure the camera to capture images in JPEG format at the highest resolution available. For best results withhighest resolution and wider color range, shoot with RAW format (NEF, CR2, PEF, etc.), as most of the photogrammetry tutorial suggested. This setting will also beneficial if you want to reliably document the rock art panel for the purpose of general preservation. However, it will require us to do a preprocessing step such as convert the RAW files to JPEG in the next section. Also, the color accuracy is not necessarily needed since the painting will be presented not in digital format and the color reproduction will rely on the paint mixture by the artist. Furthermore, the level of detail that we get from this processing will most likely get destroyed in the subsequent steps in the 3D modelling section.
Shooting Mode: Use Manual mode to prevent exposure level variations. Alternatively, use Aperture mode with a minimum speed of 1/80s.

Aperture: Set the aperture between f/5.6 and f/16, with f/8 typically being the optimal setting. This increases the depth of field and minimizes diffraction, which often occurs at smaller apertures. However, the appropriate aperture level largely depends on the lens quality.

Speed and ISO: Setting the speed can be challenging due to low light conditions, which may necessitate slower speeds and potentially introduce hand tremor. While a tripod can help stabilize the camera, cave topography may render it impractical. Therefore, setting the ISO to 640 and speed at 1/80s should yield acceptable results.
Shooting Scenario
Plan the shooting scenario before commencing photography. Depending on the panel and cave floor topography, begin by taking pictures parallel to the panel surface.
From the starting point, capture images in a vertical direction from the top to the bottom of the panel, maintaining a 75-85% vertical overlap between photos.
Move one step in the planned direction, then start photographing from the bottom and move upward, ensuring an 85-90% side overlap with the previous photo series.
Repeat this process until the entire panel surface is covered. Upon completion, remember to retrieve all markers from the panel.

Transfer all images from the memory card to a new folder on your computer. If your computer uses an SSD for the operating system and an HDD for storage, ensure that the images are stored on the same drive as the operating system to prevent processing bottlenecks due to differing transfer rates between SSD and HDD.
Launch Agisoft Metashape and import all images into the workspace as a single chunk. This can be done by selecting 'Workflow' > 'Add Photos'.
Save the project in the root directory of the image folder by clicking the 'Save' button and assigning an appropriate file name.

Right-click on one of the photos in the Photos panel, select 'Estimate Image Quality' from the dropdown menu, and choose 'All Cameras' in the pop-up window. After processing, the Quality Column in the Photos pane will display numerical values representing the focus quality of each image. Disable any image with a quality value less than 0.5 units to exclude it from subsequent processes, as it could affect alignment quality.
Align the images by selecting 'Align Photos' from the 'Workflow' menu and setting the alignment parameters as shown in the provided image.
Add the marker reference.
Click on the Reference pane tab and select the 'Settings' button. Set the Coordinate System accordingly, ensuring the 'Marker reference' box is checked.
Detect the coded markers by selecting 'Tools' > 'Markers' > 'Detect Markers…'. In the window that appears, select 'Circular 12 bit' and leave all other values at their default parameters.

Import the CSV file containing the marker coordinates by clicking 'Import reference'. In the 'Import CSV' dialogue window that appears, set the coordinate system, delimiter used, and column structure accordingly.

Place the non-marker point reference by finding the photos containing the non-marker point reference (refer to Step 5). Right-click on the point location, select 'Place Marker', and click on the appropriate point name. Repeat this step until all non-marker point references are placed in several photos.
In the Reference pane, click the 'Update Transform' button.

Duplicate the chunk by right-clicking on the chunk in the Workspace pane. Rename the new chunk by adding 'pre-optimization' to the chunk name. This will serve as a rollback point if the optimization procedure yields unsatisfactory results.

Optimize the Camera Alignment by click Tools > Optimize Cameras… A pop-up window will appear where you can select camera calibration parameters. By default, Agisoft Metashape checked f, k1, k2, k3, cx, cy, p1, and p2. In the advanced setting, check ‘Estimate tie point covariance’ and ‘Fit additional corrections’. Click OK.
Remove weak tie points

Select ‘Reconstruction Uncertainty’. Fill the ‘Level’ field with value of 10. Make sure that the selected points are not over 50% of the total of tie points. If more than 50% of tie points are selected, reduce the value by 1 units increments. Hit ‘OK’ and delete the selected points by pressing Delete on the keyboard. Optimize the camera alignment by repeating Step 15.

Open the gradual selection and select ‘Projection Accuracy’. Set the level to 3 units. Reduce this value by 0.1 units increments if more than 50% of the tie points are selected. Press OK. Delete the selected points and repeat Step 15.

Open the gradual selection and select ‘Reprojection Error. Set the level to 0.3 units or about 10% of the points are selected. Increase the value if more than 10% of the points are selected. Press OK and delete the selected points by pressing delete on the keyboard. Repeat Step 15 with a slight modification. This time, check all of the camera alignment parameters and ‘Fit additional corrections. Hit ‘OK’.
Generate a dense cloud by selecting 'Workflow' > 'Build Dense Cloud…'. Set the 'Quality' to High, 'Depth Filtering' to Moderate, and check 'Calculate point colors' and 'Calculate point confidence'.
Duplicate the chunk and assign an appropriate name.
Activate the new chunk by double-clicking the chunk name in the 'Workspace' pane. The activated chunk will be displayed in bold font.

Delete all unnecessary point clouds. Use free-form selection from the toolbar to select and delete all unnecessary points from the dense point cloud. Fine-tune the shape edge by zooming in and deleting the pointy edge of the panel point cloud. Continue this process until you achieve the desired panel shape.
Build a mesh by selecting 'Workflow' > 'Build Mesh…', which will create a mesh with open geometry.
Build a Textured Model by selecting 'Workflow' > 'Build Textured Model'.

Export the model by selecting 'File' > 'Export Model' and choosing '.obj' and '.stl' as file types. Assign an appropriate name and save location for the model. The '.obj' file is a model with its texture, useful as a reference when painting the rock art panel replica. The '.stl' file is the base open geometry that will be processed in the following section.
Launch Meshmixer and import the STL files. Meshmixer 3.5 Windows 11 Autodesk

Set the model unit Navigate to 'Actions' > 'Units' in the menu bar. Set one of the dimensions to meter (m) and click 'Done'. A pop-up window will appear; select 'Keep (x, y, z) the same'. This will translate the units to meters while preserving the value.

Transform the model orientation Navigate to 'Edit' > 'Transform'. Set the View cube to Front, and use the colored arches and arrows to rotate and move the object. Adjust until the front side of the model aligns with the front side of the view. Click 'Accept' once finished.
Smooth the boundary:
Click on the 'Select' side-toolbar and zoom in until you can double-click the blue line around the model. This will select all the connected triangle meshes around the model boundary.
Highlight the 'Modify' menu and select 'Optimize Boundary'. Then select 'Expand Ring' two to three times to expand the selected mesh inward for about 10 cm. Again, select 'Optimize Boundary'.
In the 'Select' menu, click 'Modify' > 'Smooth Boundary'. Adjust the value parameters until the object boundary is smoothed. Click 'Accept' once finished.
With the boundary still selected, click on 'Deform' > 'Smooth' (Optional). Change the parameters as needed until all of the boundaries are almost flat.

Extrude the model In the 'Select' menu, click anywhere on the model's front side to select it. Then, highlight the 'Modify...' and click on 'Select All'. With all the model surface selected, click on 'Edit' > 'Extrude'. Carefully consider the scale at which you want to print your replica. For example, if you want to downscale it to a scale of 1:2, consider giving a 20 cm extrusion. However, if you plan to print it at real size, give it a 10 cm extrusion for print stability. Modify the other parameters as needed to avoid non-manifold geometry.
Export the model Export the model to '.stl' file format for further processing in the next section. Save it and assign a distinctive name to differentiate it from other STL files.
Open Fusion 360 and import the STL file Navigate to 'File' > 'Open', then select and open the STL file saved in the previous section.

Adjust the model's orientation If required, modify the model's orientation to ensure it's correctly aligned for the splitting process. This can be done by selecting 'Modify' > 'Move/Copy' and using the handles to rotate and position the model.
Partition the model We will use the 'Split Body' tool to divide the model into smaller sections.
Begin by creating a plane for the initial split. Navigate to 'Construct' > 'Offset Plane', select a face of the model, and set the offset distance to define the location of the first split.
With the plane created, go to 'Modify' > 'Split Body'. Select the model as the 'Body to Split' and the plane you just created as the 'Splitting Tool'. Click 'OK' to execute the split.
Repeat these steps, creating new offset planes and using them to split the model, until the entire model is divided into 20 x 20 cm sections.
Create half-cylinder pegs To fortify the joints between parts, we will create half-cylinder pegs.
Navigate to 'Create' > 'Cylinder' and draw a cylinder on one of the split faces. Set the diameter and height according to the size of the pegs you want to create.

With the cylinder created, select 'Modify' > 'Split Body'. Choose the cylinder as the 'Body to Split' and a plane that bisects the cylinder as the 'Splitting Tool'. Click 'OK' to split the cylinder into two halves.
Delete one half of the cylinder to leave a half-cylinder peg. Repeat this process to create pegs on all the parts where necessary.
Place the Pegs and Create Holes for the Joints To strengthen the joints between parts, we will place the half-cylinder pegs at the joints and create corresponding holes.
Navigate to 'Insert' > 'Insert Mesh' and select the STL file of the half-cylinder peg. Position the peg at the desired joint location using 'Modify' > 'Move/Copy'.

With the peg positioned, we will create a hole in each part of the joint. Select 'Modify' > 'Combine'. In the 'Combine' window, select one part of the joint as the 'Target Body' and the peg as the 'Tool Body'. Set the operation to 'Cut' and ensure 'Keep Tools' is checked. This will create a hole in the part that matches the shape of the peg.
Repeat the 'Combine' operation for the other part of the joint, again using the peg as the 'Tool Body'. This will create a matching hole in the second part.
Repeat these steps for all joints in the model. Ensure that the pegs are correctly positioned and that the holes in each part of the joint align perfectly.

Export the Modified Parts With the pegs placed and the holes created, the parts are ready for printing. Select each part individually, navigate to 'File' > 'Export', and choose the STL format. Assign a distinctive name to each part to avoid confusion during the printing process. Repeat this step for all parts.

Open PrusaSlicer and Import the Model Parts Launch PrusaSlicer and import the STL files of the model parts. Navigate to 'File' > 'Open', then select and open the STL files saved in the previous section.
Set the Slicing Parameters Configure the slicing parameters according to the specifications.
Filament Type: Navigate to 'Filament Settings' and select 'PLA'.
Nozzle Diameter: Navigate to 'Printer Settings' > 'Extruder 1' and set the 'Nozzle diameter' to 0.6mm.
Infill: Navigate to 'Print Settings' > 'Infill' and select 'Lightning' from the 'Fill pattern' dropdown menu. Set the 'Fill density' to 20%.
Layer Height: Navigate to 'Print Settings' > 'Layers and perimeters' and set the 'Layer height' to 0.4mm.

Supports: Navigate to 'Print Settings' > 'Support material' and check 'Generate support material'. Adjust the 'Overhang threshold' and 'Enforce support for the first' parameters as needed based on the model's geometry.
Position the Model Parts Position the model parts so that the joint is standing on the print bed. You can do this by selecting the part and using the 'Place on Face' tool to adjust its position.

Slice the Model Parts With the slicing parameters set, you can now slice the model parts. Click on the 'Slice Now' button. PrusaSlicer will process the model and generate the G-code instructions for the 3D printer.
Scale and Slice the Pegs The pegs need to be slightly downscaled to ensure they fit into the peg holes on the joints.
Import the STL file of the peg into PrusaSlicer.
Select the peg and navigate to 'Object manipulation' > 'Scale'. Set the scale to 98%.
For the pegs, use the same slicing parameters as the model parts, but change the infill to 'Gyroid' at 20% and set the 'Perimeters' to 2.
With the peg scaled down, slice it by click on the 'Slice Now' button.

Export the G-code After slicing, export the G-code for each part. Navigate to 'File' > 'Export G-code'. Assign a distinctive name to each file to avoid confusion during the printing process. Repeat this step for all parts and the pegs.
Start the Printing Process With the G-code files ready, you can now start the printing process. Transfer the G-code files to your 3D printer thumb drive and initiate the print accordingly.

Join the Parts Begin by inserting the pegs into their corresponding holes on the model parts. Ensure that each peg fits snugly into its hole. If necessary, use a rubber mallet or similar tool to gently tap the peg into place.

Apply Resin Epoxy Glue To secure the parts together, apply a thin layer of resin epoxy glue to the pegs and the inside of the holes. Join the parts together according to the model's design, ensuring that the pegs align with their corresponding holes. Apply pressure to the joint until the glue sets. Repeat this process for all parts.

Smooth the Joints After the glue has fully cured, use sandpaper to smooth the joints and remove any excess glue. Start with a coarse grit sandpaper to remove larger imperfections, then gradually move to finer grits for a smooth finish.

Apply Primer Paint Once all parts are joined and the joints are smooth, apply a layer of primer paint to the entire model. This will help the paint adhere better and provide a uniform surface for the rock art replication.

Replicate the Rock Art Painting With the model primed, you can begin replicating the rock art painting. This process will largely depend on the artist's expertise and the specific techniques required for the rock art style. The artist will know the best types of paint to use and how to apply them to achieve the desired effect. Remember to allow adequate drying time between each step to ensure the best results. Always refer to the manufacturer's instructions for the specific materials you are using, such as the resin epoxy glue and paints. Please note that while this guide provides a general process for assembling and painting the model, the exact steps may vary depending on the specific model and the rock art being replicated. Always consult with experts or professionals as needed.

The size limitations of the Prusa i3MK3s necessitate the division of the model into smaller, printable parts. This section will outline the steps to partition the model into 20 x 20 cm sections using Fusion 360. To preserve the intricate details of the model's surface, we will work directly with the mesh, avoiding conversion to any other format. Additionally, we will incorporate half-cylinder pegs to reinforce the joints between parts. Fusion360 Windows 11 Autodesk

After segmenting the model into printable parts and preparing the joints, the next step is to slice the model parts using PrusaSlicer and start the printing process. Slicing is the process of converting a 3D model into instructions that the 3D printer can understand. The following steps will guide you through the slicing process with the specified parameters: Filament type - PLA; Nozzle diameter - 0.6mm; Infill - Lightning 20%; Layer height - 0.4mm; Supports - where needed. PrusaSlicer 2.7.1 Windows 11 Prusa Research
